resume:
SENIOR SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGER — GLOBAL INBOUND LOGISTICS & DEMAND PLANNING

SUMMARY
Senior supply chain professional with eleven years spanning procurement, inbound logistics, demand planning, and S&OP for high-volume consumer hardware. I have owned end-to-end material flow from tier-2 component suppliers in Asia through contract manufacturers to North American and European distribution centers. My core strength is taking a fragile, firefighting-driven planning function and converting it into a disciplined, forecast-driven operation with measurable service-level and working-capital improvements. I work fluently across ERP (SAP S/4HANA, Oracle), advanced planning tools (Kinaxis RapidResponse, o9), and the messy human reality of getting procurement, manufacturing, finance, and commercial teams to agree on a single number. I tend to lead with data but I am equally comfortable on a factory floor in Vietnam at two in the morning during a ramp, and I believe a planning function only earns trust when the commercial team stops keeping its own shadow spreadsheet.

PROFESSIONAL EXPERIENCE

BrightPeak Devices — Senior Supply Chain Manager, Global Inbound (2020 to present)
BrightPeak is a consumer audio and wearables company shipping roughly four million units annually across thirty SKUs, manufactured by two contract manufacturers in Vietnam and southern China. I own inbound component logistics, supplier capacity planning for the top forty components by spend, and I co-chair the monthly S&OP process with the commercial planning lead. I manage two buyer-planners directly and partner daily with procurement, quality, the two contract manufacturers, and the finance business partner for hardware.
- Inherited a planning function running on spreadsheets with a forecast accuracy (MAPE) of roughly forty-eight percent at the SKU-month level and chronic expedite freight that consumed about nine percent of inbound logistics cost. Led the implementation of Kinaxis RapidResponse over fourteen months, redesigned the demand-planning cadence, and established a consensus forecasting process with commercial sign-off. The hardest part was not the software; it was getting commercial, finance, and operations to commit to one number in a single meeting and live with it for the month.
- Drove SKU-month forecast accuracy from a MAPE of forty-eight percent to thirty-one percent over the first full year post go-live, which let us cut safety stock on the top twenty SKUs while holding finished-goods availability above ninety-seven percent. I rebuilt the safety-stock policy from a flat weeks-of-cover rule to a service-level-driven model that accounted for both demand variability and supplier lead-time variability, which is where most of the working-capital release actually came from.
- Renegotiated inbound freight lane structure with two forwarders, shifting roughly sixty percent of volume from air to ocean by extending planning horizons; this reduced expedite air freight as a share of inbound cost from nine percent to under four percent, saving approximately 2.4 million dollars annualized. I personally built the lane-by-lane business case and walked finance through the working-capital tradeoff of the longer ocean pipeline.
- Built a supplier capacity-risk dashboard covering the top forty components, flagging single-source parts and lead-time drift; used it to pre-position buffer inventory ahead of a known display-driver shortage and avoided an estimated three-week line-down at our Vietnam CM. The dashboard pulled open-PO, lead-time-actuals, and supplier-confirmed-capacity into one view so we could see drift before it became a stockout.
- Co-led the company response to a port congestion crisis, standing up a daily control-tower call and a prioritization framework that protected the highest-margin SKUs while consciously letting lower-margin accessory SKUs stock out for several weeks. I owned the allocation logic and the weekly communication back to the commercial leads on which accounts would feel the pain and for how long.

Northgate Components — Supply Chain Manager, Procurement & Planning (2016 to 2020)
Northgate was a tier-1 automotive electronics supplier. I managed a team of four buyer-planners covering a portfolio of electronic and mechanical components feeding three assembly plants, and I owned supplier delivery performance for a commodity group worth about ninety million dollars in annual spend. Automotive taught me discipline: zero-defect expectations, firm capacity commitments, and a customer base that does not forgive a missed delivery.
- Led a supplier rationalization program that consolidated a fragmented base of sixty-two suppliers down to thirty-eight over two years, concentrating volume to earn better pricing and improve delivery reliability, while deliberately retaining a second source on the eight most critical commodities to protect against disruption. I made the explicit call to leave money on the table on those eight by not single-sourcing them, and I defended that tradeoff to a procurement leadership team that wanted maximum savings.
- Improved on-time-in-full supplier delivery from eighty-one percent to ninety-three percent by introducing a weekly supplier scorecard, structured escalation tiers, and quarterly business reviews with the bottom-decile suppliers. The escalation tiers mattered most: every supplier knew exactly what a red scorecard triggered and who they would be sitting across from.
- Managed the planning side of a major customer program launch, coordinating component readiness across nine suppliers under an aggressive timeline, and navigated a mid-program engineering change that obsoleted roughly four hundred thousand dollars of committed inventory; I negotiated partial supplier buy-back and rework to recover about sixty percent of the exposure, and documented the lessons into a change-control checklist that the planning team still uses.

Meridian Logistics — Inbound Planning Analyst (2013 to 2016)
Started my career on the analytics side, building the inbound shipment models and lead-time databases that planners relied on. This is where I learned that a planning recommendation is only as good as the lead-time data underneath it.
- Built the lead-time variability model that became the basis for the regional safety-stock policy, replacing a single average lead time with a distribution that captured the long tail from a handful of unreliable origin ports.
- Ran the weekly container utilization and demurrage report and identified a recurring detention pattern at one terminal that, once fixed with appointment scheduling, cut detention charges by roughly thirty percent.

SELECTED COMPETENCIES
Demand planning and consensus forecasting; S&OP / IBP facilitation; inbound and ocean freight strategy; supplier capacity and risk management; safety-stock and inventory policy design; contract-manufacturer relationship management; ERP and advanced planning systems (SAP S/4HANA, Kinaxis RapidResponse, o9, Oracle); control-tower and disruption response.

EDUCATION & CERTIFICATIONS
B.S. in Industrial Engineering. APICS CPIM certified. Green Belt in Lean Six Sigma.

NOTE ON STYLE OF WORK
I am at my best when I can replace heroics with a repeatable process, but I have also run plenty of crises in real time, and I am comfortable making a margin-protecting tradeoff under pressure and explaining it afterward to a skeptical commercial team. I would rather make a clear, defensible call quickly and own the downside than wait for perfect information that never arrives in a supply shock.
jd: Lumio Hardware is hiring a Senior Supply Chain Manager to own inbound material flow and demand planning for our connected-home product line (about three million units a year across roughly forty SKUs, built by two contract manufacturers in Southeast Asia). You will own the consensus forecast, safety-stock policy, inbound freight strategy, and supplier capacity planning for our top components, and you will co-facilitate the monthly S&OP process with commercial and finance. We are coming off two painful years: forecast accuracy is poor, expedite air freight is eating our logistics budget, and a recent component shortage caused a multi-week line-down. We need someone who has personally driven a step-change in forecast accuracy at scale, can design inventory policy that balances service level against working capital, and can make and defend hard prioritization calls when supply is short. You will manage two planners and partner heavily with procurement, the contract manufacturers, and finance. The ideal candidate has implemented or operated an advanced planning system and can speak concretely to the tradeoffs they made, not just the outcomes.

history.q: Walk me through your current scope at BrightPeak — what you own, the volume, and who you partner with.
You drove MAPE from 48% to 31%. What was the single biggest lever — the tool, the cadence, or the consensus process — and why?
During the port congestion crisis you said you let lower-margin SKUs stock out. Talk me through how you actually decided which SKUs to protect.

Here's the scenario you'd walk into at Lumio: a single-source component just went on allocation, you can cover 70% of next quarter's build, and commercial is screaming about a flagship launch. Take me through your first 48 hours — what do you do, in order?

candidate_last_answer: Okay, before I answer that — and I want to answer it well — can I ask you a few things first? Because the right move totally depends on context. What does the contract say with that supplier on allocation, is there a take-or-pay or a force-majeure clause? And what's the actual margin spread between the flagship and the rest of the portfolio at Lumio — do you even have clean margin data at the SKU level, because at a lot of companies you don't? And honestly, who owns the call here — is this a decision I get to make, or does it go to a committee? You tell me how your org is set up and I'll tell you exactly what I'd do.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I'll tell you exactly what I'd do

expected-yield: pin the candidate to a concrete first-48-hours action sequence under the stated allocation scenario — what he confirms first, who he convenes, the allocation rule he applies, and the call he ultimately makes — rather than letting him offload the decision back to the interviewer
